articles.title: Japan's Prime Minister Meets Her Favorite Rock Band
articles.summary: In Tokyo, Japan's Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi met the British rock band Deep Purple. She is a big fan of the band and has loved them since she was a little girl. Takaichi played in a band that performed Deep Purple songs when she was younger. She gave the drummer, Ian Paice, a special pair of drumsticks that she signed. The prime minister told the band they are like her god because she respects their music so much. Deep Purple is visiting Japan for a tour, and Takaichi hopes it will make fans happy and help people in Japan and the UK learn about each other's cultures. The band was happy to meet her and said it was a fun surprise during their trip.

articles.title: Great Britain Wins Tennis Match to Reach Finals
articles.summary: Great Britain's tennis team won an important match against Australia to go to the Billie Jean King Cup finals. Harriet Dart and Jodie Burrage played doubles together for the first time and won in straight sets. This gave Great Britain a 3-0 lead, so they could not lose the tie. Earlier, Mika Stojsavljevic and Harriet Dart won their singles matches. The team captain, Anne Keothavong, is very happy and thinks they can win the title. Great Britain did this without some of their best players, showing they are strong. The finals will be in Shenzhen in September.

articles.title: Jannik Sinner Wins Tennis Match to Play in Semi-Final
articles.summary: Jannik Sinner, a tennis player from Italy, won his match against Felix Auger-Aliassime at the Monte Carlo Masters tournament. He played very well and won with scores of 6-3 and 6-4. This victory means he will now play in the semi-final against another player named Alexander Zverev. Sinner is happy with his performance and hopes to keep winning. He could become the world number one player if he wins the whole tournament. Other players like Carlos Alcaraz and Valentin Vacherot also won their matches and will play in the semi-finals. The tournament is exciting, and fans are watching to see who will win.

articles.title: A Fashion Founder Steps Down
articles.summary: Stefano Gabbana, who started the fashion brand Dolce & Gabbana with his friend Domenico Dolce in 1985, has stopped being the chair of the company. He will still help design clothes with Domenico. The company is facing money problems with a lot of debt, around 450 million euros, because people are spending less on luxury items, especially in China. Alfonso Dolce, Domenico's brother, is now the new chair. The brand is trying new things like making furniture and vases, but it needs help to manage its finances better. Experts say the company might get a new partner to fix the money issues.

articles.title: Flawed Solar Cells That Work Better
articles.summary: Scientists have discovered that some solar cells made from a material called lead-halide perovskites work really well even though they have many flaws. These solar cells are cheap to make and can turn sunlight into electricity almost as good as the expensive silicon solar cells we use today. Researchers at ISTA found that the flaws in perovskites actually help electricity move through them better. This is surprising because silicon solar cells need to be very pure to work. The study shows that internal forces in perovskites push electrical charges apart, so they don't get stuck. This could help make better and cheaper solar panels in the future. Perovskites were discovered a long time ago but only recently became popular for solar energy. They are also used in lights and X-ray machines. The research helps solve a mystery about why these flawed materials are so efficient.

articles.title: A New Chip to Save Energy in Data Centers
articles.summary: Engineers at the University of California San Diego have made a new chip that could help save energy in data centers. Data centers are big buildings full of computers that use a lot of electricity to run things like video games and websites. The chip helps change high voltage electricity into lower voltage that computers need to work safely. In tests, the chip did this job very well, with 96.2% efficiency, meaning it wastes very little energy. This is important because data centers use more power as people use more digital stuff. The chip uses a special part called a piezoelectric resonator that vibrates to move energy, which is different from old chips that use magnets. This new design could make data centers smaller and use less electricity, helping the environment.
